Notion vs Obsidian: Which Is Better?

Choosing between Notion and Obsidian? Here's a side-by-side comparison to help you decide.

Quick verdict: Choose Notion if you need freelancers or solo-devs. Choose Obsidian if you prioritize freelancers or writers.

Notion

PricingFree / $10/mo Plus / $18/mo Business
Rating★★★★½ 4.5/5

Key Features

  • Block-based editor that lets you build anything from docs to databases
  • Relational databases with rollups, formulas, and linked views
  • 200+ templates across every use case you can imagine
  • API and integrations with Slack, Zapier, Figma, and more
  • Team collaboration with comments, real-time editing, and permissions
  • AI assistant for writing, summarization, and Q&A (paid add-on)

Pros

  • Molds to whatever workflow you throw at it — kanban boards, wikis, databases, calendars, you name it
  • Templates are genuinely good; the community template gallery alone is worth exploring
  • Free tier is shockingly generous for what you get (unlimited pages, blocks, and collaborators)
  • The block-based editor means you can nest and organize content in ways no other tool lets you
  • Collaboration is smooth — real-time editing, comments, and sharing all work without friction

Cons

  • Drags noticeably once you push past ~200 database entries, especially on mobile
  • No offline access on mobile is a real pain when you're on a train or plane
  • Database queries are surprisingly limited compared to something like Airtable
  • The more you customize, the harder it is for teammates to navigate — there's no guardrails
  • Exporting out of Notion to another tool is messy; they don't exactly make it easy to leave
Try Notion →

Obsidian

PricingFree / $25/mo Commercial use
Rating★★★★½ 4.6/5

Key Features

  • Local Markdown-first storage — your notes are plain files, not a database
  • Bidirectional links and graph visualization for discovering connections between ideas
  • 1,500+ community plugins for customization (Kanban, spaced repetition, mind maps)
  • Canvas whiteboard for visual thinking and spatial arrangement
  • Obsidian Publish for turning your notes into a public website
  • Obsidian Sync for encrypted, cross-device syncing

Pros

  • Everything lives on your machine as plain Markdown files — no lock-in, no syncing drama, no format anxiety
  • Plugin ecosystem is massive (1,500+ community plugins) and covers everything from Kanban to mind maps to Pomodoro timers
  • The graph view is surprisingly useful for seeing connections between ideas you'd forgotten about
  • Canvas feature lets you visually arrange notes, images, and links in a freeform whiteboard space
  • Obsidian Sync is genuinely fast and encrypted if you do want cloud access

Cons

  • Syncing between devices requires Obsidian Sync ($5/mo) or doing it yourself with iCloud/Git
  • Steep learning curve — you'll spend hours setting up plugins and workflows before it clicks
  • Mobile app is functional but feels rough compared to the desktop experience
  • The community plugins are great but can break on updates, leaving you hunting through forums at 11 PM
  • No built-in real-time collaboration; it's fundamentally a single-player tool
Try Obsidian →

Which Should You Choose?

Both Notion and Obsidian are excellent tools in their category. The right choice depends on your specific needs:

  • Choose Notion if: If you've ever wished your note-taking app could also be your project manager, database, wiki, and to-do list rolled into one, Notion is probably what you're looking for. It's become the default workspace for a huge chunk of the freelance world — and for good reason.
  • Choose Obsidian if: Obsidian is what happens when you make a note-taking app for people who think deeply about how they think. It stores everything as local Markdown files — no proprietary format, no cloud lock-in — and lets you build a personal knowledge graph by linking your notes together. It's not trying to be Evernote. It's trying to be a second brain.
We may earn a commission if you purchase through links. All opinions are our own.
